name: Ashanti
description: The Ashanti (Asante) are the dominant Akan sub-group of central Ghana and the seat of the Ashanti Kingdom — one of the wealthiest and most militarily powerful states in West African history. Their capital Kumasi (Kumase) rose to prominence in the 17th century under Osei Tutu I, who united Akan clans around the Golden Stool (Sika Dwa Kofi), the sacred symbol of Ashanti nationhood. They are renowned for kente cloth, gold-weight casting, and the Odwira festival.
cultural_highlights: Golden Stool (Sika Dwa) — the sacred symbol of Ashanti nationhood, believed to contain the sunsum (spirit) of the entire Ashanti nation; never sat upon
Kente cloth — hand-woven silk and cotton fabric with specific patterns reserved for royalty and ceremony; a global symbol of African identity
Gold-weight (abrammoo) — brass weights used to measure gold dust, each depicting proverbs and social wisdom
Odwira festival — annual festival of purification and harvest, featuring the Asantehene (king) in full regalia
Adinkra symbols — a visual language of 90+ symbols encoding philosophical and spiritual concepts, printed on cloth and carved in architecture

food_traditions.name: Fufu and light soup
food_traditions.description: Pounded cassava/plantain with clear palm-based soup — the Ashanti national dish and a marker of hospitality

food_traditions.name: Kontomire stew
food_traditions.description: Cocoyam leaf stew with eggs or smoked fish — a staple green vegetable dish of the forest zone

food_traditions.name: Kelewele
food_traditions.description: Spiced fried ripe plantain — a street food snack and party appetiser beloved across Ghana

food_traditions.name: Palm nut soup
food_traditions.description: Deeply flavoured palm nut cream soup with chicken or bushmeat — the richer cousin of light soup

music_traditions: Fontomfrom royal drums — massive royal drums reserved for the Asantehene; their sound announces the king's presence
Kete court music — ensembles of flutes, horns, and drums performed exclusively for royal ceremonies
Highlife — the guitar-and-brass popular music style that spread from Ghana to all of West Africa in the 20th century
